description: Derivation Concepts with clinical naming AND explicit relationships to dimensional concepts. In this variant, each category and concept declares its structural relationship to the dimensional concepts — which dimensions are required, which are optional, and what role each plays (partition, constraint, order). The Transformation inherits these relationships and only adds what is specific to the clinical context.
designRationale: In v0.20a and v0.20b, dimensional concepts are listed as available but the relationship between a derivation concept and its dimensions is left to the Transformation. This means every Transformation repeats the same dimensional bindings (Subject, Parameter, Visit) even when they are structurally determined by the concept. In v0.20c, the concept carries these relationships, so the Transformation only declares what the concept does not already know.
namingPrinciples: Would a clinical programmer call this data by this name without further context?
Does the name describe what the data IS, not how or why it was produced?
Can you say the name without referencing a parameter, visit, or standard?
Would you handle this data differently than any other concept?
Could you define it in one sentence without referencing another concept?
Is it a different ingredient type, not a different brand of the same ingredient?

dimensionalConcepts.note: These are concepts in the model, but they are not derivation concepts. They define the context in which a derivation is performed. The Implementation maps them to physical columns (e.g. USUBJID → Subject, PARAMCD → Parameter, AVISIT → Visit). Some dimensional concepts have qualifiers — semantically different versions that a transformation must choose between.

Subject.definition: The individual for whom the derivation is computed.

Parameter.definition: The measure or endpoint being derived.
Parameter.note: PARAM/PARAMCD/PARAMN are representations (label vs code vs number), not qualifiers. They are the same concept in different encodings.

qualifiers.definition: Whether timing is relative to an event or absolute calendar

values.Relative: Expressed as days/hours from a reference event

qualifiers.definition: Whether the value is protocol-defined or analysis-derived

values.Protocol: As defined in the study protocol
values.Analysis: As derived for analysis purposes (e.g., windowed visits)

qualifiers.definition: Whether the value reflects planned assignment or actual receipt

Population.note: One flag per population — no qualifier needed.

qualifiers.definition: Whether the value reflects planned assignment or actual receipt

dimensionalRoles.note: When a concept declares a relationship to a dimensional concept, it specifies the role that dimension plays in the derivation.
dimensionalRoles.partition: The dimension defines a group. The derivation is computed independently within each partition. The dimension is preserved in the output.
dimensionalRoles.constraint: The dimension imposes a structural rule. For example, Comparison requires that both inputs share the same Parameter.
dimensionalRoles.order: The dimension provides the ordering axis. The derivation summarises across this axis — it collapses in the output.
dimensionalRoles.scope: The dimension defines the boundary within which the derivation operates, without partitioning or ordering.

PointComputation.definition: A value computed at a specific point, without reference comparison, temporal aggregation, event aggregation, or classification.
PointComputation.inputPattern: Method-driven. The concept defines output shape only; the method determines what inputs are needed.

Parameter.note: Each input and output Measure has a Parameter. Inputs may have different parameters (e.g. WEIGHT, HEIGHT for BMI), and the output may create a new one (BMI).

Measure.definition: A value produced by applying a computation to one or more source values.

result._note: The valueType determines physical representation: Quantity → numeric (AVAL/--STRESN), CodeableConcept/string → character (AVALC/--STRESC). SDTM unit standardisation (--ORRES → --STRESN) is a data pipeline concern, not a concept qualifier.

Comparison.definition: Data expressing the relationship between two values of the same parameter.
Comparison.inputPattern: Concept-driven. Requires 2× Measure inputs, intrinsic to the category.

Parameter.note: Both inputs must share the same parameter. The output inherits it.

Timing.note: The two inputs exist at different time points; the output sits at the source timepoint.

Change.definition: The arithmetic difference between two values of the same parameter.

PercentChange.definition: The proportional difference between two values of the same parameter, expressed as a percentage.
examples: percent change from baseline in tumor size

Ratio.definition: The quotient of two values of the same parameter.

LogRatio.definition: The logarithm of the quotient of two values. Symmetric, additive scale.

Shift.definition: The categorical movement between two values of the same parameter.

SequenceAggregate.definition: A summary of an ordered series of the same parameter over time.
SequenceAggregate.inputPattern: Concept-driven. Requires 1× Measure input; the ordered series emerges from dimensional scoping.

Parameter.note: The series is of a single parameter. Inherited by the output.

Timing.note: Provides the ordering axis. COLLAPSES in output — the summary is across all timepoints.

Period.note: May define the window over which the series is summarised.

PeakValue.definition: The highest value in an ordered series.

TroughValue.definition: The lowest value in an ordered series.

TimeToPeak.definition: The time at which the highest value occurs.

TimeToTrough.definition: The time at which the lowest value occurs.

CumulativeTotal.definition: The running total accumulated over time.

RateOfChange.definition: The rate at which a value changes over a time segment.

HalfLife.definition: The time for a value to decline by half.

LastValue.definition: The last observed value in an ordered series.

FirstValue.definition: The first value in an ordered series.

TimeInState.definition: The total time spent above or below a threshold.

TimeToThreshold.definition: The time at which a series first crosses a defined threshold.

Range.definition: The difference between the highest and lowest values in a series.

EventAggregate.definition: A summary of discrete event occurrences on a timeline.
EventAggregate.inputPattern: Concept-driven. Requires 1× Observation (OC layer); the event set emerges from dimensional scoping.

Parameter.note: The event type (e.g. specific AE term). Output typically gets a new parameter.

Timing.note: Provides the timeline over which events are counted. Not an ordering axis — events are discrete occurrences.

Period.note: May define the window in which events are counted.

EventCount.definition: The number of times an event or condition occurs.

EventRate.definition: The number of occurrences per unit of time or exposure.

TimeToEvent.definition: The duration from a defined start to event occurrence or censoring.

TimeBetweenEvents.definition: The duration between consecutive recurrent events.

Classification.definition: A derivation mapping value(s) to a categorical assignment.
Classification.inputPattern: Concept-driven. Requires 1× any concept as input (the value being classified).

Parameter.note: The classification may be parameter-specific (e.g. responder flag per parameter) or create a new parameter (e.g. population flag).

Timing.note: Preserved when classifying at each timepoint.

Flag.definition: A binary indicator evaluating a criterion (Y/N).

Category.definition: A categorical grouping derived from rules.

Rank.definition: The ordinal position of a value within a defined scope.

QuantileGroup.definition: A distributional bin assignment (tertiles, quartiles, quintiles).
QuantileGroup.math: Qₖ where F(x) in bin k

transformationExample_v020c.note: This shows how a Transformation simplifies when the concept carries dimensional relationships. Compare with v0.20a/b where the Transformation must declare all dimensional bindings.

T.ChangeFromBaseline.note: The Transformation only adds AnalysisVisit. Subject and Parameter (with the same-parameter constraint) are inherited from the Comparison category.

T.PeakConcentration.note: The Transformation adds nothing. All dimensional relationships are inherited from the SequenceAggregate category. The concept already knows it partitions by Subject and Parameter and collapses the Timing axis.

inheritedFromConcept.Subject: partition (cardinality 1)
inheritedFromConcept.Parameter: partition — each input Measure carries its own Parameter (WEIGHT, HEIGHT)

T.BMICalculation.note: Parameter is inherited and present on both inputs, but each input has a different parameter value (WEIGHT, HEIGHT). The output creates a new parameter (BMI). Compare with Comparison, where Parameter is a constraint (both inputs must share the same parameter). In PointComputation, inputs can have different parameters — the method determines how they combine.
